title: Low-Temperature Suppression of Intertwined Orders in La$_{1/3}$Sr$_{2/3}$FeO$_{3}$ Thin Films
authors: Mayia A. Vranas, Robin Glefke, Katherine Matthews, Xiaoke Li, Tianxing Wang, Henry Navarro, I-Ching Lin, Sarmistha Das, Biswajit Sahoo, Rourav Basak, Wei He, Holden Bauer, Ella Di Liberti, Jacob Butler, Elliot Kisiel, Erik Lamb, Jun-Sik Lee, Cheng-Tai Kuo, Heemin Lee, Christie Nelson, Sophie Morley, Sujoy Roy, Christoph Klewe, Fanny Rodolakis, Jessica McChesney, Oleg Shpyrko, Yimei Zhu, Eric Fullerton, Ivan Schuller, Alex Frano
abstract: The strong coupling between spin, charge, and lattice degrees of freedom in perovskite oxides leads to an array of exotic phenomena, giving these materials rich phase diagrams that can include coupled orders. This is exemplified by the A-site doped ferrite La$_{1/3}$Sr$_{2/3}$FeO$_{3}$ (LSFO), which exhibits a coupled paramagnetic-antiferromagnetic and charge ordering phase transition at $\sim$190 K that has been well studied in thin films, bulk, and polycrystalline samples. However, the low temperature behavior of LSFO thin films below $\sim$100 K has not been thoroughly explored. This work uses several X-ray scattering and spectroscopy techniques to directly probe LSFO's magnetic and charge order down to low temperature. Using resonant X-ray scattering, we observe a complete suppression of LSFO's known antiferromagnetic and charge order below $\sim$25 K. Further spectroscopy and coherent scattering measurements provide insight into LSFO's electronic structure and domain dynamics in this new low temperature phase, and we propose possible explanations for the observed order suppression based on reduced dimensionality of domains in our thin films. Our findings provide insight into the effects of competing interactions in strongly correlated materials, particularly those with coupled orders.
summary: This work uses synchrotron X-ray scattering to investigate the low-temperature phase diagram of LSFO thin films, which exhibit coupled magnetic and charge ordering. The primary finding is the sharp suppression of both orders below 25 K, suggesting the system enters a novel, less ordered ground state. The research highlights the critical role of dimensionality and competing interactions in determining the final electronic state of strongly correlated perovskites.
main_problem: The study investigates the low-temperature behavior of intertwined magnetic and charge orders in La1/3Sr2/3FeO3 (LSFO) thin films, focusing on the regime below 100 K.
main_result: The paper reports the complete suppression of both the antiferromagnetic and charge ordering signals below approximately 25 K, suggesting a complex, low-temperature ground state.
method: The research employs advanced X-ray scattering and spectroscopy techniques, including Resonant X-ray Scattering (RSXS), X-ray Photon Correlation Spectroscopy (XPCS), and X-ray Magnetic Circular Dichroism (XMCD).
model_or_system: The system is the perovskite oxide La1/3Sr2/3FeO3 (LSFO) grown as epitaxial thin films on various substrates. The material exhibits strong coupling between spin, charge, and lattice degrees of freedom, leading to coupled magnetic and charge ordering.
key_observables: Temperature dependence of AFM and CD Bragg peak amplitudes, out-of-plane correlation lengths, dynamic timescales ($	au$) from XPCS, and valence state ratios from XAS.
important_parameters: Low temperature regime ($<100 	ext{ K}$), critical suppression temperatures ($T^* \sim 60 	ext{ K}$, $T_{	ext{supp}} \sim 20 	ext{ K}$), and film thickness/dimensionality effects.
main_assumptions: The order suppression is intrinsic to the LSFO film, ruling out substrate-induced effects, and the low-temperature phase is not ferromagnetic.
figures_summary: Figures show temperature dependence of magnetic/charge peaks (RSXS), correlation length analysis (CD stable, AFM decreases), and XPCS waterfall plots illustrating dynamic changes near transition temperatures.
paper_structure: The paper systematically uses multiple techniques (RSXS, XPCS, XMCD, XAS) to map the phase transitions, first establishing the high-temperature coupled order, then tracking its decay upon cooling, and finally proposing that the collapse is driven by the instability of the long-range AFM order in reduced dimensions.
why_interesting: The study provides detailed experimental evidence of how competing interactions and reduced dimensionality can destabilize complex, coupled electronic orders in strongly correlated oxides, which is a key area of modern condensed matter physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. LSFO Structure and Order. a) Crystal structure of LSFO, modified from [11]. b) Schematic of the CD (indicated by cation color) and AFM order (indicated by arrow direction) in LSFO. c) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of a cross-section of LSFO grown on an LSAT(100) substrate. The Sr/La and Fe cation sites are labeled with blue and orange circles, respectively.

FIG. 2. Resonant X-Ray Scattering Results. a) Horizontal scattering geometry of our soft resonant X-ray scattering (RSXS) experiments. Hard resonant X-ray scattering (REXS) was done in a vertical scattering geometry instead. b,f) Energy level diagrams of the electronic excitations for our Fe L3 edge RSXS and Fe K edge REXS measurements, respectively. c,g) Temperature dependence of the amplitudes of LSFO’s AFM and CD Bragg peaks, ⃗q = � 1

FIG. 4. Low Temperature Magnetic and Electronic Probes. a) Fe L2,3 edge XMCD measurements at three temperatures between 22 K and 220 K. b) Fe L2,3 edge X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) of LSFO at three temperatures taken in luminescence yield (LY). The L-edge peaks are labeled according to excited core state (L3 - 2p3/2; L2 - 2p1/2) and final crystal field split valence states (3d eg and t2g)[8, 26]. c) The difference in absorption spectra from 220 K to 120 K (orange) and from 120 K to 18 K (blue), highlighting the changes in spectral weight across both transition temperatures. d) Field-cooled (FC) and zero field-cooled (ZFC) moment vs temperature measurements of LSFO. e) Difference...

FIG. 5. X-ray Photon Correlation Spectroscopy (XPCS) of the AFM Peak. a) A schematic of the coherent scattering process, in which a coherent beam scattering off of magnetic domains in the sample creates a speckle pattern that varies over time. b) Waterfall plots for four temperatures, where a line cut across the Bragg peak is plotted for every frame recorded during measurement, showing the evolution of speckled in time. c) The intermediate scattering function, g1, plotted for four temperatures. The low temperature and high temperature dynamics show different timescales. d) Temperature-dependent contrast of speckles for six exposure times, showing an increase in contrast at low temperatures.
